justification: Judicial institutional trust is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning perceptions of system functioning (p22), judicial independence (p41), fairness of trials (p42_1), Supreme Court fairness (p46), equal treatment (p33), and accountability (p50), and no single item captures the full construct. PCA is preferred over sum/mean scoring because the items use heterogeneous scale widths (K=5 to K=9), making raw summation distort the composite; the first principal component will weight items by their shared variance and produce a single latent score that can be binned into 5 quantile categories. p43 is excluded because it measures a normative preference (justice vs. legalism) rather than trust per se, reducing construct contamination.

Legal cynicism and endorsement of extrajudicial justice is a latent attitudinal construct that manifests across multiple behavioral and normative scenarios—vigilante punishment (p17), extrajudicial killing (p18), tolerance of system errors (p19), dispute resolution preferences (p27), and executive defiance of courts (p44)—so no single item is sufficient. Scale widths vary (K=6 to K=8) and item content spans distinct facets of cynicism, making PCA the most appropriate method to extract the dominant shared dimension while accommodating scale heterogeneity. All five items have reasonable entropy (H≥1.79) and strong face validity for the construct, justifying their joint inclusion.

Police trust and legitimacy spans affective trust (p38_1), respect (p24_1), perceived fairness (p25a_1), and procedural justice expectations (p40_2_1), requiring aggregation to capture the full construct. p59 is excluded because it asks about preferred policy mechanisms to prevent abuse—a prescriptive opinion rather than a trust or legitimacy evaluation—which would introduce construct-irrelevant variance. Scale widths are heterogeneous (K=4 to K=13), so PCA is preferred over summation to extract the shared latent dimension without distortion from differing response ranges.

Legal self-efficacy and access is best captured by items reflecting subjective competence and perceived utility of the legal system: rights knowledge (p12), belief that suing the government is possible (p36), likelihood of winning such a suit (p37), and whether going to court is worthwhile (p39). p28 is excluded because it measures behavioral intention under a hypothetical constraint rather than efficacy beliefs, p34–p35 tap specific amparo knowledge which is a narrow legal literacy sub-domain, and p54 reflects cost-preference rather than efficacy. The retained items share a common evaluative orientation toward personal legal agency and use comparable ordinal scales (K=6–7), making PCA aggregation appropriate.

p7 directly asks respondents why they obey laws, making it the gold-standard face-valid measure of compliance motivation—the construct's core theoretical content—by eliciting the underlying normative, instrumental, or coercive reasons for compliance. The remaining items measure perceived law-abidingness of others (p8, p9, p11_1) or self-reported behavior (p10), which are correlates of compliance motivation but not its direct operationalization; mixing them would conflate motivation with descriptive norms and self-reported behavior. Although p8–p10 have higher entropy, their content validity for motivation is lower, and a single best-match item is preferable to an aggregate that blurs conceptually distinct phenomena.
